# Hadrukh Formation

**Summary**: Geological formation studied in the thesis collection.

---

## How It Appears in the Thesis Collection

- [[2012-iwalewa|Iwalewa, 2012]] studied the Hadrukh Formation

- [[2018-mohamed|Mohamed, 2018]] part of the sedimentary sequence in the eastern part of Saudi Arabia.

- [[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025]] investigated the mineralogical properties of local formations for carbon storage.

- [[2002-al-ali|Al-Ali, 2002]] analyzed Hadrukh Formation samples to characterize local lithology.

- [[1995-hasan|Hasan, 1995]] analyzed the Hadrukh formation as a component of the Neogene aquifer system.

## Depositional Environment



- [[2018-mohamed|Mohamed, 2018]] part of the sedimentary sequence in the eastern part of Saudi Arabia.

- [[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025]] investigated the mineralogical properties of local formations for carbon storage.

- [[2002-al-ali|Al-Ali, 2002]] analyzed Hadrukh Formation samples to characterize local lithology.

- [[1995-hasan|Hasan, 1995]] analyzed the Hadrukh formation as a component of the Neogene aquifer system.

## Reservoir Properties



## Key Findings Across Sources

- studied the Hadrukh Formation ([[2012-iwalewa|Iwalewa, 2012]])

- The Hadrukh Formation consists of sandstones and marls. ([[2018-mohamed|Mohamed, 2018, p. 37]])

- Not stated in source (Note: The thesis focuses on 'Saudi Basalt' generally, but specific local names may be linked to these units). ([[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025]])

- The formation is approximately 20 to 120 meters thick and dates from Lower Miocene to early Burdigalian. ([[2002-al-ali|Al-Ali, 2002, p. 65]])

- The Hadrukh formation has a thickness of approximately 50 m and exhibits high primary intergranular porosity with yields up to 50 l/sec. ([[1995-hasan|Hasan, 1995, p. 60]])
